---
title: "Censoring, Truncation & Selection"
subtitle: "When the outcome is observed only partly"
---

## Overview

Sometimes the problem is not the *shape* of the outcome but the *way it is observed*. Out-of-pocket spending is recorded as zero for everyone who sought no care, masking how negative their "demand" really was. Wages are seen only for people who chose to work. Survey income is top-coded. In each case the sample we analyse is a distorted window onto the population, and ordinary regression --- which takes the observed values at face value --- returns biased answers.

This module sorts out the three related phenomena. Censoring (the outcome is pulled to a limit) and truncation (observations beyond a limit vanish entirely) are handled by the Tobit and truncated-regression models. Sample selection --- where *whether* we observe the outcome depends on a separate, outcome-correlated process --- is the subtler and more dangerous case, addressed by the Heckman correction and its all-important exclusion restriction. The thread connecting them, and to the rest of the book, is that valid inference requires modelling the missingness, not ignoring it.

## Pages in This Module

- [Censoring, Truncation, and the Tobit Model](censoring-truncation-tobit.qmd) --- When the outcome is observed only partly.
- [Sample Selection: The Heckman Model](heckman-selection.qmd) --- When who you observe depends on the outcome.
